title: Linux Networking Processes
theory: Active programs run as processes managed by PIDs. You monitor system threads using `ps` and `top`, and terminate them using `kill`. The ampersand `&` spawns background jobs listed via `jobs` and foregrounded using `fg`. Network interface adapter settings are listed via `ip a`, gateway tables via `ip route`, host lookups using `dig`, and open server listening ports using `ss` or `netstat`. Services are started and reloaded via `systemctl` scripts.

interactive_examples.description: Exposes CPU/RAM usage metrics of all running system processes.
Spawns a sleep process running persistently in the background.
Lists active TCP listening sockets and matching port bounds.

exercises.description: Find the PID of uvicorn using `ps aux | grep uvicorn`.
Fetch remote web headers using `curl -I https://www.google.com`.

quiz.question: Which tool lists active network adapters and IP configurations?
How do you launch a command as a background job?

lessons.title: 1. Terminal Processes (ps)
lessons.definition: Processes are active running instances of programs in Linux.
lessons.explanation: The ps command lists processes running in your current terminal session.
lessons.instruction: Print the processes running in this terminal session.

lessons.title: 2. System Processes (ps aux)
lessons.definition: ps aux prints all running processes across the whole system.
lessons.explanation: a (all users), u (detailed fields), x (processes not attached to terminals).
lessons.instruction: Expose details of all running processes in the system.

lessons.title: 3. Real-time Monitoring (top)
lessons.definition: top displays a real-time, dynamic view of active running processes.
lessons.explanation: Outputs CPU cycles, RAM consumptions, uptime details, and active threads.
lessons.instruction: Query process dynamic parameters using top.

lessons.title: 4. Terminate Processes (kill)
lessons.definition: The kill command sends signals to terminate running processes.
lessons.explanation: Requires a target Process ID (PID). SIGKILL (-9) forces immediate shutdown.
lessons.instruction: Terminate a process with PID 9999 (use simulation standard return).

lessons.title: 5. Background Jobs (sleep &)
lessons.definition: The ampersand (&) executes a command in the background.
lessons.explanation: Keeps the terminal responsive for more commands while it executes.
lessons.instruction: Run a sleep command for 100 seconds in the background.

lessons.title: 6. Job Controller (jobs)
lessons.definition: The jobs command lists background processes spawned by this shell.
lessons.explanation: Shows status (running, stopped) and job number identifiers.

lessons.title: 7. Bring to Foreground (fg)
lessons.definition: fg moves background jobs to the foreground, letting them take terminal focus.
lessons.explanation: Takes a job identifier parameter, for example, fg %1.
lessons.instruction: Bring background job 1 back to the foreground.

lessons.title: 8. Network Interfaces (ip a)
lessons.definition: ip address (ip a) displays local network adapters and IP configurations.
lessons.explanation: Used to verify local adapter status, loopbacks, and network parameters.

lessons.title: 9. Gateway Routes (ip route)
lessons.definition: ip route outputs network routing lookup tables.
lessons.explanation: Exposes destination gateways, interfaces, and gateway routing parameters.

lessons.title: 10. Connectivity Ping
lessons.definition: ping sends ICMP ECHO_REQUEST packets to verify connection status.
lessons.explanation: Validates if a remote host is alive and responding.

lessons.title: 11. Active Sockets (ss)
lessons.definition: ss dumps socket statistics, replacing the older netstat tool.
lessons.explanation: Shows active listening TCP/UDP ports, matching connection routes.

lessons.title: 12. DNS Lookup (dig)
lessons.definition: dig performs DNS record resolution lookups.
lessons.explanation: Queries name servers for A, MX, CNAME, and TXT mapping parameters.
lessons.instruction: Query DNS record details for google.com.

lessons.title: 13. HTTP Requests (curl)
lessons.definition: curl transfers data from or to a server using supported protocols.
lessons.explanation: Commonly used to test API services or download configurations.
lessons.instruction: Fetch headers from an endpoint using curl.

lessons.title: 14. Systemd Services
lessons.definition: systemctl manages systemd services status and boot processes.
lessons.explanation: Can inspect, launch, reload, disable, or stop daemon services.
lessons.instruction: Check the status of the nginx service.

lessons.title: 15. Restarting Daemons
lessons.definition: systemctl restart stops and immediately starts a system service.
lessons.explanation: Commonly executed after updating configuration files to reload parameters.
lessons.instruction: Restart the nginx service to reload configurations.
